source_paper_title: Efficient Approximation Schemes for Stochastic Probing and Selection-Stopping Problems
context:
Section 7 (Future Directions), page 34

An immediate open question for future research is whether one or more of these problems admit fully polynomial time approximation schemes (FPTAS). That is, for any constant \(\epsilon > 0\), can we obtain a \((1 + \epsilon)\)-approximation in \(\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)\) time?
title: Existence of an FPTAS for Free-Order Prophets, ProbeMax, and related stochastic optimization problems
latex:
Given an input size parameter \(n\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1}\), consider any one of the following stochastic optimization problems studied in the paper: (i) Free-Order Prophets, (ii) non-adaptive ProbeMax, (iii) adaptive ProbeMax, or (iv) Pandora’s Box with Commitment.

In each problem instance, there are \(n\) independent nonnegative random variables \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) with explicitly given distributions (in whatever standard input model is assumed for that problem, e.g., discrete support with rational probabilities, and with the ability to compute the expectations required by the objective). Let \(\mathrm{OPT}\) denote the optimal expected objective value for the corresponding problem instance.

The problem is to determine whether there exists a fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS) for any (or all) of these problems. Formally, does there exist an algorithm \(\mathcal{A}\) such that for every accuracy parameter \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), and every instance \(I\) of the problem with input length \(|I|\), the algorithm runs in time \(\mathrm{poly}(|I|,1/\varepsilon)\) and outputs a feasible solution with expected objective value at least \((1-\varepsilon)\,\mathrm{OPT}\) (for maximization problems)?

Equivalently: decide whether the problems admit approximation algorithms whose running time is polynomial both in the instance size and in \(1/\varepsilon\), rather than merely \(t(\varepsilon)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(|I|)\) for some potentially superpolynomial \(t(\varepsilon)\) (as in an EPTAS).
open_problem_quote: An immediate open question for future research is whether one or more of these problems admit fully polynomial time approximation schemes (FPTAS). That is, for any constant \(\epsilon > 0\), can we obtain a \((1 + \epsilon)\)-approximation in \(\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)\) time?

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
